name: Little loop to discover Pau
name_original: Petite boucle à la découverte de Pau
region_intro: Touring cycling around Pau offers diverse terrain, from the foothills of the Pyrenees to river valleys and vineyard-covered hillsides. The region is characterized by the majestic Pyrenees mountains to the south, providing challenging ascents and panoramic views. The Gave de Pau river flows through the area, offering flatter routes along its banks. Southwest of the city, the Jurançon hillsides present rolling terrain ideal for scenic rides through vineyards. Best touring cycling routes around Pau The most popular touring cycling route is Little loop to discover Pau, a 6.4 miles (10.2 km) trail that takes 38 minutes to complete, offering an easy exploration of the city's surroundings. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Jurançon Hillsides Trail, a difficult 44.8 miles (72.1 km) path winding through vineyards with views of the Pyrenees. Local touring cyclists also love the Historic loop between Pau, Tarbes and Lourdes, a 42.8 miles (68.8 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 4 hours 13 minutes. Touring cycling around Pau is defined by the dramatic Pyrenees mountains, the gentle paths along the Gave de Pau river, and the picturesque Jurançon vineyards. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside rides to demanding mountain ascents. The routes in Pau are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 700 reviews. More than 9,000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Pau's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Pau?
A: There are over 1000 touring cycling routes in the Pau region, offering a wide variety of options for all skill levels. The komoot community has explored more than 1060 routes, with 268 easy, 428 moderate, and 366 difficult options.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Pau?
A: Pau offers incredibly diverse terrain for touring cyclists. You can find challenging mountain ascents in the majestic Pyrenees, gentle and flat paths along the Gave de Pau river, and rolling hillsides covered in vineyards in the Jurançon region. This variety ensures routes suitable for different preferences and fitness levels.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Pau?
A: The touring cycling routes around Pau are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 780 reviews. Cyclists often praise the stunning views of the Pyrenees, the well-maintained paths along the Gave de Pau, and the picturesque rides through the Jurançon vineyards.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Pau?
A: Yes, Pau offers many easy touring cycling routes perfect for beginners or families. There are 268 easy routes available. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e861787656/little-loop-to-discover-pau" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Little loop to discover Pau&lt;/a&gt;, which is just over 10 km long and takes less than an hour to complete, offering a gentle introduction to the area's surroundings. Another family-friendly choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15012742" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pont d'Assat Suspension Bridge – Gave de Pau loop from Idron&lt;/a&gt;, which follows the river for about 26 km.

Q: Where can I find more challenging touring cycling routes near Pau?
A: For experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, the Pau region, particularly towards the Pyrenees, offers numerous difficult routes. There are 366 difficult routes available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e860363457/jurancon-hillsides-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jurançon Hillsides Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for example, is a demanding 72 km route through vineyards with significant elevation changes. For even more intense climbs, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40961898" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soulor Pass – Old Betharram bridge loop from Coarraze-Nay&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/28979898" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col du Soulor – Old Betharram bridge loop from Montaut-Bétharram&lt;/a&gt; will test your endurance with substantial ascents into the Pyrenees foothills.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes that are circular around Pau?
A: Many touring cycling routes around Pau are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e861787656/little-loop-to-discover-pau" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Little loop to discover Pau&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e868923738/historic-loop-between-pau-tarbes-and-lourdes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic loop between Pau, Tarbes and Lourdes&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/36324105" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gave de Pau – Old Betharram bridge loop from Pau&lt;/a&gt;. These loops offer diverse scenery from cityscapes to riverbanks and historical towns.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along touring cycling routes in Pau?
A: Pau's touring cycling routes often pass by significant landmarks and natural features. You can admire the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4119926" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;National Museum and Estate of the Château de Pau&lt;/a&gt;, enjoy panoramic views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1109052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boulevard of the Pyrenees&lt;/a&gt;, or cycle past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5354510" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pau whitewater stadium&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features impressive natural wonders like the Grottes de Bétharram, which are accessible along some cycling paths. For a route that includes a view of the Château, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38533712" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Whitewater stadium bridge – View of Château de Pau loop from Pau&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Pau?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Pau is generally from spring to autumn. During these seasons, the weather is mild and pleasant, ideal for exploring the diverse landscapes. Spring brings blooming vineyards and lush riverbanks, while autumn offers comfortable temperatures and beautiful fall foliage. While winter cycling is possible, routes in the higher Pyrenees might be affected by snow or colder conditions.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes in Pau that are accessible by public transport or have parking facilities?
A: Pau is a cycling-friendly city with good infrastructure. Many routes are accessible from the city center, which has public transport links. For routes starting outside the city, parking is generally available in nearby towns and villages. The Vélosud (V81) cycle route, which follows the Gave de Pau, is well-integrated with public transport options, connecting Pau with towns like Lourdes. You can find more information on public transport access along the Vélosud route on &lt;a href="https://en.francevelotourisme.com/cycle-route/the-velosud/pau-lourdes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;France Vélo Tourisme&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find amenities like cafes or accommodation along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, the Pau region is well-equipped for cyclists. Many touring routes pass through charming towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and local shops to refuel. The region's reputation as a cycling haven means many accommodations cater specifically to cyclists, offering secure bike storage and other services. The Jurançon vineyards also offer unique opportunities for wine tasting at local wineries along the routes.

Q: Are there any touring cycling routes that follow the Gave de Pau river?
A: Yes, the Gave de Pau river offers several gentle and scenic touring cycling routes. These paths are known for their accessibility and beauty, connecting Pau with charming medieval towns. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15017474" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Whitewater stadium bridge – Gave de Pau loop from Pau&lt;/a&gt; provide refreshing rides along the riverbanks. The Vélosud (V81) is a prominent cycle route that also follows the Gave de Pau, offering a dedicated and well-maintained path.

Q: Are there any specific routes that offer views of the Pyrenees mountains?
A: Absolutely. The Pyrenees form a dramatic backdrop to Pau, and many routes offer stunning views of the mountains, including the iconic Pic du Midi d'Ossau. Cycling through the Jurançon hillsides, for instance, on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e860363457/jurancon-hillsides-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jurançon Hillsides Trail&lt;/a&gt;, provides picturesque vistas of the Pyrenees. For direct engagement with the mountains, routes heading south from Pau will take you into the foothills and offer increasingly dramatic scenery.

Q: Are there any touring cycling routes that pass through vineyards?
A: Yes, the Jurançon hillsides southwest of Pau are famous for their vineyards, and several touring cycling routes wind through this beautiful AOC region. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e860363457/jurancon-hillsides-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jurançon Hillsides Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering picturesque views of rolling terrain blanketed in vines, with the Pyrenees in the distance. These routes also provide the unique opportunity to stop for wine tasting at local wineries.
tips: The staircase leading to the footbridge (left bank) has a single rail very close to the ramp to slide the wheels of the bike. It's not very comfortable with a normal bike (it's heavy) but it's completely unacceptable for loaded bikes, tricycles, trailers, as well as for wheelchairs 😩

Funny story this Gave de Pau, also called Gave de Gavarnie from its source in Villelongue, Gave de Pau or Lavedan from this confluence in Ayzac-
Ousse then to the two islands of Labatut, including Lourdes and Pau
then again Gave de Pau (but without the Lavedan) then to Cauneille and finally
Gaves reunited to Horgave!
